coredata.dc:title: Blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots prepared from zeolite as efficient sensors for picric acid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bolun
Mu, Ying
Zhang, Chenghui
Li, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: The excitation-dependent blue photoluminescent and oxygen-rich carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been prepared through calcinations and etching from MgAPO-44 zeolite occluding N-methylpiperidine organic template. The as-prepared CNDs, with about 3.4nm in diameter, possess excellent dispersibility and photostability in both water and ethanol solution. These CNDs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity for picric acid (PA) in detection of nitro and nitrophenol compounds in water and ethanol solution with the detection limit as low as 0.36μM and Ksv of 1.30×105. Detection effect can be influenced by the concentration and pH value of CNDs in water solution. Study reveals that the efficient and fast detection of PA can be attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).
coredata.dcterms:subject: CNDs
MgAPO-44 zeolite
Fluorescent sensor
PA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. PXRD patterns of simulated CHA zeolite (bottom) and experimental MgAPO-44 (up); b. Illustration of CNDs confined in MgAPO-44 zeolite; c. TEM and HRTEM images of CNDs (insert: size histograms of CNDs); d. FTIR spectrum of as-prepared CNDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The survey and C 1s, N 1s and O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CNDs.

a. UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs (insert: photographes of the CNDs under illumination of white light and 365nm UV light); b. excitation–emission matrix (EEM) of CNDs water solution; c. FL spectra of CNDs in water with different PA concentrations of 0–63.75μM (inset: CNDs solution without and with 63.7μM PA under the 365nm UV light); d. Stern-Volmer plot of PA in water solution (Inset: enlarge view of a selected area); e. FL spectra of CNDs in ethanol with different PA concentrations of 0–60.0μM (inset: CNDs solution without and with 60.0μM PA under the 365nm UV light); f. Stern-Volmer plot of PA in ethanol solution (Inset: enlarge view of a selected area).
a. The PL intensity of CNDs water solution in different phenols; b. The PL intensity of CNDs ethanol solution in different nitro compounds.
UV–vis spectra of all the phenols and nitros and the emission spectrum of CNDs.
Comparision of sensing ability of as-prepared CNDs with other detectors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CS=compounds species; GQDs=graphene quantum dots; MOFs=metal-organic Frameworks; QDs=quantum dots; CNDs=carbon nanodots; TbCDs=terbium doped carbon dots; AIEMM=AIE luminogen-functionalized mesoporous materials; GCNs=graphitic carbon nanosheets.

head.ce:title: Blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots prepared from zeolite as efficient sensors for picric acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excitation-dependent blue photoluminescent and oxygen-rich carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been prepared through calcinations and etching from MgAPO-44 zeolite occluding N-methylpiperidine organic template. The as-prepared CNDs, with about 3.4nm in diameter, possess excellent dispersibility and photostability in both water and ethanol solution. These CNDs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity for picric acid (PA) in detection of nitro and nitrophenol compounds in water and ethanol solution with the detection limit as low as 0.36μM and Ksv of 1.30×105. Detection effect can be influenced by the concentration and pH value of CNDs in water solution. Study reveals that the efficient and fast detection of PA can be attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) as versatile fluorescent nanoparticles have attracted great interest owing to their excellent optical properties, facile synthesis, outstanding aqueous solubility, low bio-toxicity and environmental protection compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots [1–3]. Thus, CNDs have widespread applications in bioimaging, medical diagnosis, photovoltaic and sensors [4–10]. As for the sensors or probes, CNDs can be used extensively in detecting various inorganic and organic species, such as metal ions (i.g. Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Be2+, Ag+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+ [11–18]), hypochlorous acid [19], glucose [20], dopamine [21], uric acid [22], ssDNA [23], melamine [24], and so on.

Recently, the explosive detection becomes more important and urgent for the society safety, as well as the environmental protection. As a primary class of explosive, nitroaromatic explosives usually include nitrobenzene (NB), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), 4-nitrochlorobenzene (4-NCB), p-nitrotoluene (NT), 3-nitrotoluene (3-NT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), picric acid (PA) and so on. Among them, PA is a common chemical reagent and industrial organic compound, which is widely used in pesticides, pigments, dyes and pharmaceuticals [25–27]. However, a little amount of PA can cause serious pollution to the soil and aquatic systems [28–30]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop a facile and efficient method for sensitively and selectively detecting PA among various nitroaromatic explosives with high similarity.

The fluorescence (FL) sensing with the advantages of fast, simple and non-destructive operation has become the dominant means in various methods employed in PA detection [31–35]. Many materials including curcumin and its derivatives [36], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and lanthanides-doped nanoparticles are selected as PL probes of PA in different solutions. Such examples include a supramolecular Tröger’s base derived coordination zinc polymer with detection range of 0–90.9μM and a quenching constant of 4.37×104 M−1 in aqueous suspension [37]; a terbium-doped blue carbon dots with the detection range of 500nM–100μM and a detect limit of 200nM in water solution [38], and recyclable AIE luminogen-functionalized mesoporous materials with a quenching constant of 2.5×105 M−1 and a detection limit of 0.4ppm in water solution [39].

Compared with other FL sensors, the advantages of CNDs are obvious, which include simple synthesis, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and excellent photostability. It is noted that the different preparation approaches of CNDs may result in the production of CNDs with distinct fluorescent property, structure, composites and/or surface groups, which will further affect the sensing ability of CNDs for detecting various species. In this work, the oxygen-rich CNDs have been prepared from the pyrolysis of N-methylpiperidine (NMD) confined in MgAPO-44 zeolite with CHA topology. The as-prepared CNDs show blue and excitation-dependent emission with the best emission at 430nm. Moreover, these CNDs have good solubility and photostablility in water and ethanol solution, which can be used as a PL sensor for sensitive and selective determination of PA. This work provides a quick and efficient method for sensing PA in both water and ethanol solution.

According to the previous work [40], heteroatom-containing aluminophosphate MgAPO-44 zeolite was synthesized by hydrothermal method in a reaction system of MgO–Al2O3–P2O5–N-methylpiperidine (NMD)–H2O. First, pseudoboehmite (Al2O3, 62.5%) and magnesium acetate tetrahydrate (Mg(CH3COO)2·4H2O, 99.0%) were dispersed in water solution then with the addition of orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85wt%). Finally, NMD (C6H13N, 99.0%) was added to this mixture. The reaction mixture, with a gel ratio of 1.0 Al2O3: 1.0 MgO: 2.2 P2O5: 5.0 N-methylpiperidine: 350H2O, was stirred at room temperature for about one hour. Then the gel was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 3 days. The final product containing square-like crystals was washed in distilled water and dried at 60°C for 12h.

The as-synthesized MgAPO-44 zeolites was placed into a normal furnace and heated from room temperature to 450°C at a temperature ramp rate of 2°Cmin−1 in air, followed by a 4h isothermal hold at this temperature and then cooled down to room temperature with the same temperature ramp rate. The organic template NMD confined in MgAPO-44 zeolite was carbonized during this process. The calcinated sample was dissolved in 2.5ml of 2.5M NaOH aqueous solution at room temperature. Then the solution was placed in ultrasonic treatment for about 60min to accelerate the dissolution. Finally, 5ml ethanol was added into the solution and centrifuged at a speed of 10,000r/min for 30min to separate insoluble. The dark supernatant was collected and filtered by 0.22μm filter membrane. Filter and centrifuging may be repeated several times to get the pure clear and transparent solution. The as-prepared yellow-brown solution was dried in rotary evaporator to obtain CNDs solid for further characterization. The water and ethanol solutions of CNDs were prepared at a concentration of 0.75mg/ml for further use.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were collected on a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was measured on a Bruker FTIR IFS-66V/S. A baseline correction was applied after measurement. UV–vis adsorption spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin F20 transmission electron microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo Escalab 250 spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. Photoluminescence spectra were recorded on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin-Yvon). Nano-second fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using Edinburgh F900 fluorimeter.

For the detection of PA in water solution with different pH value, three buffet solutions with different pH values were configured. In detail, for pH=5 (HAc, NaAc), 5.4g sodium acetate trihydrate was dissolved in 50ml distilled water, next pH value was adjusted to 5.0 with acetic acid, then diluted with distilled water to 100ml; for pH=7 (KH2PO4, NaOH), potassium phosphate monobasic 0.68g was dissolved in 29.1ml 0.1mol/L NaOH solution, then diluted to 100ml; for pH=9 (H3BO3, NaOH, KCl), boric acid 3.09g was dissolved in 500ml 0.1mol/L KCl solution, then 210ml 0.1mol/L NaOH solution was added. Five phenols, including picric acid (PA), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2.4-dinitrophenol (DNP), phenol and hydroquinone (HQ) were dissolved in distilled water to afford 2mg/ml aqueous solution. All samples were prepared at room temperature. In a typical detection experiment, different amount of CNDs solution with a concentration of 0.75mg/ml (10–150μl) was added into a colorimetric cuvette, followed by 2ml buffet solution with different pH value. All the phenols were added by 1μl each time.

Nine nitro compounds including nitrobenzene (NB), p-nitrobenzaldehyde (NBA), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), 4-nitrochlorobenzene (4-NCB), p-nitrotoluene (NT), 3-nitrotoluene (3-NT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (PA) were dissolved in ethanol with a concentration of 2×10−3 mol/L. 50μl CNDs ethanol solution with a concentration of 0.75mg/ml was added to 2ml ethanol in colorimetric cuvette, afterwards each nitro compound was added into the cuvette in the same way as water solution. All samples were prepared at room temperature.

PXRD study of MgAPO-44 shows that the as-synthesized product possesses CHA zeotype structure and has a pure phase [41] (Fig. 1 a). After calcination, CNDs@MgAPO-44 composite was prepared upon carbonization of N-methylpiperidine (NMD) located in MgAPO-44 (Fig. 1b), and then CNDs were isolated from the zeolite by etching the inorganic framework used NaOH aq. Morphological characterization by TEM image shows that isolated CNDs have a typical spherical shape and are well separated (Fig. 1c). Their size distribution ranges from 2.5nm to 6.0nm, with an average diameter of 3.4nm.

The XPS spectra of CNDs are shown in Fig. 2 , which indicate that C/N/O are major elements of carbon nanodots. The C 1s spectra can be divided into two peaks of CN/CO bonds (286.2eV) and CC bonds (284.7eV). The N 1s spectra can be fitted into two peaks corresponding to CN*/NH bonds (401.8eV) and CNC bonds (399.5eV). The O 1s spectra give CO/CO bonds (532.4eV) in CNDs. Fig. 2a clearly shows that the as-prepared CNDs possess the carbogenic core with rich O-based groups. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum shown in Fig. 1d indicates three major peaks located at 3424cm−1 (OH/NH3), 1623cm−1 (CO) and 1427cm−1 (CONH2). It is noted that the N content in the as-prepared CNDs (about 1. 6% analysed from XPS spectra) is similar to the previous reports (about 2.0%) of CNDs prepared by the pyrolysis of templates confined in zeolites [42], while the O content (about 66.3%) is much higher. This indicates that the pyrolysis of the organic templates confined in zeolites not only can prepare CNDs with uniform size benefitted from the well-confined pore space of zeolites, but also the tuning of the zeolite framework type, the kind of template, and calcination temperatures and times can produce the variety of CNDs with different compositions and surface groups. Thus, CNDs with interesting luminescent properties and different sensing abilities may be prepared by using this method.

The as-prepared CNDs can be well dispersed in both water and ethanol solution. A broad absorption with two shoulder peaks at 290 and 332nm is shown in UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs, which indicates π-π* and n-π* transitions (Fig. 3 a). The excitation spectrum of CNDs is shown in Fig. S1. Upon illumination at 365nm UV light, the bright blue PL of CNDs can be observed with the naked eye. The fluorescence excitation–emission matrix (EEM) of CNDs water solution is shown in Fig. 3b. It demonstrates the steady excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence of CNDs with the best emission at 430nm when excited at about 360nm. Notably, the PL property of CNDs keeps well and is stable in water solution with different pH values (Fig. S2), as well as in the ethanol solution (Fig. S3), suggesting their high photostability. In addition, the as-prepared CNDs have good solubility and stability in water and ethanol, which is stable for over six months, and only slight PL intensity changes are observed (Fig. S4).

Owing to the photoluminescent properties and excellent photostability of CNDs in water and ethanol, we assume that the as-prepared CNDs could be used as a PL probe for detecting PA. The detection experiments are performed in both water and ethanol. In water solution of CNDs, as shown in Fig. 3c, the significant fluorescence quenching can be observed with the addition of PA from 0μM to 63.75μM. The emission peak shows a slight red-shifting from 430nm to 460nm with the increasing amount of PA, which verifies the electrostatic inter-actions and hydrogen bonding between N-based groups of CNDs and hydroxyl groups of PA [43]. The fluorescence Stern-Volmer curves can be perfectly fitted to an exponential equation of F0/F=1.32×e[PA]/32.08 −0.34 with a R2 =0.9998 (Fig. 3d). The calculated detection limit (DL) is 0.36μM and Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) is 1.30×105 M−1, which is comparable with the detection performance of other carbon nanoparticles and quantum dots (Table 1 ).

Furthermore, the response time of CNDs on PA in water solution has been investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, PL spectra of CNDs can be quickly quenched in only 30s, then the PL spectra remains the same with the increasing time from 30s to 20min. This indicates that PA can be detected by CNDs rapidly.

Interestingly, the pH value and concentration of CNDs water solution have diverse effects on the PA detection. Fig. S6 shows the PL quenching and Stern-Volmer plots of CNDs in water solution with different pH values. When the pH value is changed from 5 to 9, their fitting equations are F0/F=1.17×e[PA]/30.64 −0.18, F0/F=1.32×e[PA]/32.08 −0.34, and F0/F=1.18×e[PA]/30.26 −0.15, respectively. Their calculated Ksv increases slightly, from 1.28×105 to 1.30×105 to 1.34×105 M−1. This indicates that the electrostatic inter-actions as well as hydrogen bonding between N-based groups of CNDs and hydroxyl groups of PA are enhanced under basic conditions, leading to a more significant quenching. Further study indicates that the sensing ability for PA is decreased in the concentrated water solutions of CNDs. The calculated Ksv decreases from 1.37×105 to 1.17×105 M−1 when the addition volume of CNDs solution increases from 10μl to 150μl (Fig. S7). This may be due to fluorescence’s self-absorption of as-prepared CNDs.

On the other hand, CNDs also display sensing ability for PA in ethanol solution. With the increasing PA amount from 0μl to 60μl, the fluorescent intensity of CNDs gradually decreases, resulting in a great quenching effect of luminescent intensity. The Stern-Volmer curve is fitted by exponential equation of F0/F=1.72×e[PA]/54.28 −0.76 with a R2 =0.9944. The detection limit of ethanol solution is 0.22μM and Ksv is 5.7×104 M−1 (Fig. 3e and f).

To further investigate the selectivity of CNDs on PA detection, different nitro compounds are evaluated. Owing to the solubility, five phenol compounds including picric acid (PA), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2.4-dinitrophenol (DNP), phenol and Hydroquinone (HQ) with a concentration of 63.75μM were used for selectivity experiments in water solution. Fig. 4 a shows that five nitro phenols can weaken the photoluminescent intensity of CNDs in different degrees with the efficiency order of PA>4-NP>DNP>phenol>HQ. The F/F0 value of PA is much lower than other phenols, indicating a high selectivity for PA in water solution (Fig. 4a).

On the other hand, nine different nitro compounds are used to perform the comparable selectivity experiments in ethanol, which include nitrobenzene (NB), p-nitrobenzaldehyde (NBA), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), 4-nitrochlorobenzene (4-NCB), p-nitrotoluene (NT), 3-nitrotoluene (3-NT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (PA) with 68.80μM solutions (Fig. 4b). These compounds had different quenching effect on the PL intensity of CNDs. The detecting performance of CNDs on PA, 4-NP and DNP are better and obvious than others in ethanol. PA presents the minimum F/F0 value of 14.5%, which is much lower than DNP (30.0%) and 4-NP (56.5%).

Usually, the quenching process of CNDs can be explained in two possible reasons: fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and molecular inter-actions. As shown in Fig. 5 , there is an evident spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PA and the emission spectrum of CNDs from 350 to 450nm, which permits the energy transfer from the excited state of CNDs to the ground state of PA. In this condition, the as-prepared CNDs could selectively and sensitively detect PA in solution via electrostatic interaction between CNDs and PA. The amino groups and other N-based groups of CNDs bind with hydroxyl groups of PA via electrostatic inter-actions and hydrogen bonding, leading to the significant quenching. Also, when high concentrations of PA are added into the aqueous solution of CNDs, red shifting is observed in emission peak, confirming the presence of molecular interactions between PA and CNDs. Moreover, the lifetimes for CNDs remain almost the same before and after adding PA (Fig. S8), which indicate a static quenching process and further confirms the strong FRET occurring between CNDs and PA. Compared with PA, other nitros have a shorter absorption peak wavelength which cannot be enough overlapped with CNDs’ emission spectra, so they could not be efficiently detected.

We make a functional application of CNDs detection of PA. First, strip filter paper is soaked by the as-prepared CNDs solution and then dried in air. Then, 30μl distilled water is put on the first one and 30μl PA solution of 2×10−3 M on another. The obvious colour difference can be observed when the dried filter paper illuminated under 365nm UV light, the first filter paper is bright, but the second one is dark (Fig. S9). The results show that CNDs prepared in this paper are simple and efficient sensors for PA detection.

A new kind of excitation-dependent blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots that can be well dispersed in both water and ethanol solution have been synthesized by the pyrolysis of N-methylpiperidine occluded in the MgAPO-44 zeolite. The as-prepared CNDs have oxygen-rich surface, as well as uniform spherical shape and size in the range of 3.0–5.0nm. The rapid, highly selective and sensitive detection for PA in both of water and ethanol solution endows CNDs with great potentials in environmental sensor applications. The detection limit and Ksv can achieve 0.36μM and 1.30×105 M−1, respectively. The fluorescence quenching response to PA should be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21621001, 21320102001) financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+

Quinoline derivative-functionalized carbon dots as a fluorescent nanosensor for sensing and intracellular imaging of Zn2+
High photoluminescent carbon nanodots and quercetin-Al3+ construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
Carbon dots with tunable emission, controllable size and their application for sensing hypochlorous acid
Synthesis-modification integration one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine
Synergistic electrocatalytic effect of graphene/nickel hydroxide composite for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid
Amplified fluorescent sensing of DNA using graphene oxide and a conjugated cationic
Si-doped carbon quantum dots: a facile and general preparation strategy bioimaging application, and multifunctional sensor
Colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensors for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and investigation of their co-crystal structures
Optical detection of submicromolar levels of nitro explosives by a submicron sized metal-organic phosphor material
Polymer sensors for nitroaromatic explosives detection
extraction and high performance liquid chromatography-ultra violet determination of some explosives in water samples
A novel picric acid film sensor via combination of the surface enrichment effect of chitosan films and the aggregation-induced emission effect of siloles
Sensitivity gains in chemosensing by lasing action in organic polymers
Imprinted Au-nanoparticle composites for the ultrasensitive surface plasmon resonance detection of hexahydro-1,3,5-trinitro-1,3,5-triazine (RDX)
Ion chromatography-mass spectrometry: a review of recent technologies and applications in forensic and environmental explosives analysis
Highly ordered binary assembly of silica mesochannels and surfactant micelles for extraction and electrochemical analysis of trace nitroaromatic explosives and pesticides
Selective and sensitive TNT sensors using biomimetic polydiacetylene-coated CNT-FETs
A label-free fluorescence sensing approach for selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous solution using graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Highly efficient and selective detection of picric acid among other nitroaromatics by NIR fluorescent organic fluorophores
A supramolecular Troger’s base derived coordination zinc polymer for fluorescent sensing of phenolic-nitroaromatic explosives in water
Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots
Supersensitive detection of explosives by recyclable AIE luminogen-functionalized mesoporous materials
Carbogenic nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites with modulated full-color luminescence
Luminescent carbon dots in a new magnesium aluminophosphate zeolite
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots: efficient fluorescent chemosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
A facile synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution

Microwave-assisted ultrafast and facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from a single precursor: preparation, characterization and their application for the highly selective detection of explosive picric acid
Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots as a photoluminescence sensing platform for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection
Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue–green photoluminescent carbon nanodots
N, B-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for Hg2+ ions and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection for bioimaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bolun Wang got his BS degree in chemistry from Jilin University, Changchun in 2015. He continued his graduate study at Jilin University under the supervision of Prof. Jiyang Li. His main research focused on the synthesis of carbon nanodots and their application on sensing.

Ying Mu received her BS degree from Chemical and Environmental Engineering College of Dalian Jiaotong University (PR China) in 2012. Now she is pursuing her PhD study under the supervision of Prof. Jiyang Li. Her research interests focus on carbogenic nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites and their photoluminescent properties.

Chenghui Zhang received her B.Eng. in chemical engineering and technology from Qufu normal university in 2013. She is currently a Ph. D student under the supervision of Prof. Jiyang Li at Jilin University. Her research is focused on the synthesis of photochromic metal–organic frameworks for sensing applications.

Jiyang Li is a Professor of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University at Changchun. Her research work has been focused on hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis of novel zeolitic materials; zeolite, open-framework and MOF materials with photochromic and proton conduction properties; luminescent carbon dots derived from zeolitic materials.
